title: Kundalini and Siddhi Powers: What to Do When You Start Experiencing Them
meta_description: So, you're here. Probably late at night, heart pounding, mind racing. You've been seeing things, hearing things, feeling things that just aren't... normal.
body:
Kundalini and Siddhi Powers: What to Do When You Start Experiencing Them

So, you're here. Probably late at night, heart pounding, mind racing. You've been seeing things, hearing things, feeling things that just aren't... normal. You're searching, frantically, for answers, for someone who gets it. Someone who won't look at you like you're completely unhinged. I get it. I've been there. That terrifying, exhilarating, utterly isolating place where your reality starts to unravel, and these so-called "siddhis" - these strange powers or phenomena - begin to pop up like uninvited guests at a party you didn't even know you were throwing. It's not a party, is it? It feels more like an ambush. Right?

You're probably scared. You might be questioning your sanity. You're wondering if you're going crazy, or if this is some kind of cosmic joke. Let me tell you, you're not crazy. Or, if you are, then so am I, and so are a whole lot of other people who've walked this path. This isn't some New Age fantasy. This is real. And it's happening to you. So, let's talk about it. Let's talk about what's going on, and more critically, what you can actually do to keep from completely losing your mind.

The Uninvited Guests: What Are These "Siddhis" Anyway?

You've heard the term, maybe, "siddhis." It's an old Sanskrit word, often romanticized in spiritual circles. It means perfections, attainments, powers. In ancient texts, they talk about things like levitation, omniscience, mind-reading, healing, manifesting. Sounds pretty cool, right? Like something out of a comic book. But when they start happening to you, it's not cool. It's disorienting. It's terrifying. Because you didn't ask for them. You didn't train for them. They just... appeared.

Look, I'm not here to tell you how to become a superhero. I'm here to tell you that these phenomena, whatever you want to call them - siddhis, spiritual gifts, weird shit - they're often a byproduct of a deeper energetic process. And that process, for many of us, is kundalini. Kundalini isn't just some abstract concept. It's a real, tangible energy. It's like a powerful current that's been dormant in your system, and for whatever reason - intense meditation, trauma, spiritual practice, or sometimes, just randomly - it's woken up. And when it wakes up, it starts to clean house. And that cleaning process can be messy. Really messy.

So, what might you be experiencing? It varies wildly, but here are some common ones I've heard about and, honestly, experienced myself:

  • Intuitive flashes: You just know things. About people, about events, about situations, without any logical explanation. It's not just a hunch; it's a certainty.
  • Precognition: Seeing snippets of the future, in dreams or waking visions. Sometimes they're clear, sometimes they're symbolic. You might see an accident before it happens, or know who's calling before you look at the phone.
  • Telepathy: Hearing people's thoughts, or having your thoughts received by others. This one can be particularly unsettling because it blurs the lines of your own mind.
  • Clairvoyance/Clairaudience: Seeing things that aren't physically present, or hearing sounds/voices that no one else does. Sometimes it's subtle, like a shimmer in your peripheral vision; other times, it's vivid.
  • Energy sensations: Intense heat, cold, tingling, vibrations, electrical currents moving through your body. Often concentrated in certain areas, like the spine or crown of the head.
  • Healing abilities: You might find your touch alleviates pain in others, or you might start spontaneously healing from chronic conditions yourself.
  • Out-of-body experiences (OBEs): Feeling like you're floating above your body, or even leaving it entirely. This can be incredibly disorienting, especially if you're not expecting it.
  • Manifestation: Things you think about or desire seem to appear in your life with uncanny speed. This can feel like magic, but it can also feel like a burden if your thoughts aren't aligned.

This isn't an exhaustive list, not by a long shot. The human consciousness is capable of so much more than we're usually aware of. And when kundalini starts doing its work, it can crack open those dormant capacities. It's like your internal operating system just got a major, unasked-for upgrade, and now you're running programs you don't even have a manual for. Know what I mean?

"I'm Not Crazy, Right?" - The Crisis of Reality

That's the question that echoes in your head, isn't it? "Am I losing my mind?" Because everything you thought you knew about reality, about how the world works, about who you are - it's all being challenged. You're experiencing things that science can't explain, that your friends and family can't comprehend. And if you try to talk about it, you're met with blank stares, concern, or worse, outright dismissal. You might even get told you need medication, or therapy, or both. And maybe you do! But it's not always the whole story.

Here's the thing: spiritual awakening, especially when it involves kundalini, often mirrors symptoms of psychological distress. Intense anxiety, paranoia, depression, dissociation, even psychotic-like experiences. It's why so many people going through this end up in mental health systems that aren't equipped to understand what's truly happening. They're trying to put a conventional label on an unconventional experience. It's like trying to describe a rainbow to someone who's only ever seen black and white. They just don't have the context.

I've been there. I've had moments where I genuinely thought I was fracturing, that the world was collapsing around me. The fear is primal. It's the fear of losing yourself, of being utterly alone in an experience no one else can validate. And that isolation? That's what really gets you. You feel like you're screaming into a void. You feel like you're the only one. But you're not. And that's why I'm talking to you now.

You need to understand that what you're going through, while terrifying, can also be a sign of deep transformation. It's a rebirth, but like all births, it's messy and painful. The old self, the old reality, has to die for the new one to emerge. And those "siddhis"? They're just indicators that your consciousness is expanding, that your energetic system is undergoing a massive overhaul. They're not the goal; they're side effects. Sometimes useful, often distracting, always demanding attention.

Grounding: Your Lifeline in the Storm

So, what do you do? When your world is upside down and these strange abilities are manifesting, the absolute first, most important thing you can do is ground yourself. Grounding isn't just some woo-woo concept; it's essential for maintaining your sanity and stability during this process. Think of it like an electrical circuit. If too much power flows through a system without a proper ground, it fries. Your body, your mind, your spirit - it's the same. You need to discharge excess energy, anchor yourself to the physical plane, and create a sense of safety.

Here are some things that can help, and honestly, they're not always easy when you're in the thick of it, but you've gotta try:

  • Connect with nature: Get outside. Walk barefoot on the grass or dirt. Hug a tree (seriously, it helps). Sit by water. Feel the sun on your skin, the wind in your hair. Nature has a way of absorbing and balancing energy. It's a natural reset button.
  • Physical activity: Exercise. Not just gentle yoga, though that's good too. I'm talking about getting your heart rate up. Running, dancing, boxing, anything that gets you fully into your body and moving that energy around. This helps to release pent-up energy and anxiety.
  • Eat nourishing, heavy foods: Think root vegetables, whole grains, protein. Avoid sugar, caffeine, and anything that makes you feel jittery or ungrounded. You need solid fuel for this intense process.
  • Sensory input: Engage your senses. Listen to calming music, take a warm bath with essential oils, light a candle, look at beautiful art. Anything that brings you into the present moment and away from the swirling chaos in your head.
  • Routine and structure: Even if you feel like you're floating, try to maintain some semblance of a daily routine. Regular sleep, regular meals, even small tasks. This creates a sense of predictability and safety in a world that feels unpredictable.
  • Creative expression: Write, draw, paint, play music, sing. Get that energy out through creative channels. It's a powerful way to process intense experiences and emotions without getting stuck in your head.
  • Avoid stimulants: Seriously, cut back on coffee, alcohol, drugs. They'll just increase the energetic chaos and make everything feel even more intense and disorienting.

These practices aren't a cure, but they're a life raft. They're what keep you tethered to reality when you feel like you're drifting out to sea. And believe me, you want to stay tethered. You don't want to lose your grip completely.

Seeking Support: You Don't Have to Do This Alone

This is where it gets tricky, because finding the right kind of support is crucial, and it's not always easy. You can't just talk to anyone about this. Most people won't understand, and some might even make things worse with their fear or judgment. But you absolutely cannot go through this in complete isolation.

  • Find a spiritual teacher or guide: This is probably the most important. Someone who has actually been through it. Not just read about it. Someone who understands kundalini, spiritual emergency, and these phenomena from direct experience. They can help you make sense of what's happening, provide guidance, and offer a sense of validation you desperately need. It's not easy to find them, but they're out there. Keep looking.
  • Support groups: There are online communities and sometimes local groups for people experiencing spiritual awakening or spiritual emergency. Connecting with others who share similar experiences can be incredibly validating and comforting. You'll realize you're not alone, and that's a huge relief.
  • Therapists who understand spiritual emergence: This is a specific niche, but it's growing. Look for therapists who are open to transpersonal psychology, or who have experience with spiritual crises. They might not understand the "siddhis" part directly, but they can help you process the intense emotions, anxiety, and trauma that often come with this experience. They can help you differentiate between what's spiritual and what might be genuinely psychological distress that needs professional attention. I'll tell you what, having a good therapist who doesn't dismiss your experiences is worth its weight in gold.
  • Trusted friends or family: If you have one or two people in your life who are genuinely open-minded, non-judgmental, and can just listen without trying to "fix" you, lean on them. You don't need them to understand everything; you just need them to be there.

I know it's hard to reach out. It feels vulnerable. It feels like admitting you're broken. But you're not broken. You're transforming. And transformation needs support. You might find some excellent resources in books like Spiritual Emergency: When Personal Transformation Becomes a Crisis (paid link). It's a classic for a reason, full of stories and insights that can make you feel less alone. Also, The Kundalini Experience by Lee Sannella (paid link) offers a clinical perspective on this phenomenon, which can be surprisingly grounding when you're feeling unmoored. For those seeking even more guidance, When Spirit Leaps: Navigating the Process of Spiritual Awakening by Bonnie Greenwell (paid link) and The Kundalini Guide by Bonnie Greenwell (paid link) are invaluable. Bonnie Greenwell knows her stuff, and her work provides a lot of practical advice and validation for what you're going through.

The Path Forward: Surrender and Discernment

This isn't a quick fix. This isn't a 3-step program to enlightenment. This is a journey, and it's often a long, arduous one. But you can get through it. You will get through it. And you'll come out the other side a different person, a deeper person, a more conscious person.

Two concepts are absolutely vital as you move forward: surrender and discernment.

Surrender, Not Give Up

Surrender doesn't mean giving up or becoming a doormat to whatever is happening. It means letting go of your resistance to the process. It means accepting that you're in something much bigger than yourself, something you can't control with your ego or your intellect. When you fight it, you create more suffering. When you resist the energy, it builds up and can cause more intense, sometimes painful, experiences. Surrender means saying, "Okay, I don't understand this, it's terrifying, but I'm going to allow it to unfold. I'm going to trust that there's a deeper intelligence at work here." It's about letting go of the need to control the uncontrollable.

This doesn't mean passively allowing yourself to be overwhelmed. It means actively choosing to flow with the energy, to observe it without judgment, and to trust that your system knows what it's doing, even if your conscious mind is freaking out. It's about cultivating a deep inner stillness amidst the chaos. It's about finding that calm center within you, even when everything else is roaring.

Discernment: Not Everything Is Gold

Just because you're experiencing spiritual phenomena doesn't mean every thought, every vision, every message is deep truth. Your ego is still very much present, and it loves to play tricks. It can interpret things in distorted ways, inflate your sense of self-importance, or even create delusions. This is why discernment is so crucial.

  • Question everything: Don't automatically believe every message or insight you receive. Run it through your inner wisdom. Does it feel true? Does it align with love, compassion, and common sense?
  • Check for ego inflation: Are these experiences making you feel superior to others? Are they feeding a sense of specialness that separates you? That's usually the ego talking, not genuine spiritual insight.
  • Look at the fruit: Does the experience lead to greater peace, clarity, and compassion in your life? Or does it lead to more confusion, fear, and disconnection? The true measure of any spiritual experience is its impact on your character and your ability to live a more loving life.
  • Don't get attached to the powers: These "siddhis" are side effects, remember? They're not the point. If you start focusing on developing them, or using them for personal gain, you're missing the whole point of spiritual awakening. They can become a major distraction, even a trap. The goal isn't to be powerful; it's to be conscious.

So yeah, you're going through something intense. Something few people will ever truly understand. But you're not alone. This path is ancient, and many have walked it before you. Stay grounded, seek wise counsel, and remember that these experiences, as terrifying as they might be, are ultimately guiding you towards a deeper truth about yourself and the nature of reality. It's a rough ride, but it's one that can lead to incredible freedom. Hang in there. You've got this.

This is not a substitute for professional mental health care. If you're in crisis, please reach out to a qualified therapist or call 988.
author: Kalesh
